string.text: ⚔️ Consignment from {CASTLE}: +{GOLD}g ({COUNT} of your prisoner{PLURAL} recruited by AI lords, your {SHARE}% depositor share).
⚔️ Consignment from {CASTLE}: +{GOLD}g ({COUNT} prisoner{PLURAL} enslaved at {TOWN}, your {SHARE}% depositor share).
⚔️ Consignment from {CASTLE}: +{GOLD}g ({COUNT} prisoner{PLURAL} absorbed into garrison, your {SHARE}% depositor share).
⚔️ Deposited {COUNT} prisoner{PLURAL} at {CASTLE}. You receive {SHARE}% of processing income (holding fee: {FEE}%).
Manpower: cannot recruit {TROOP} — {POOL} needs {COST}, only {LEFT} left.
No elite troops available. Pool regenerates daily from castle manpower.
Castle recruit screen failed to open: {ERR}
You have no prisoners to deposit.
No troops or prisoners available yet. Elite troops regenerate daily from the castle's manpower pool.
[Campaign++] Load a campaign first to see the full compatibility report.
No other gameplay mods detected. Campaign++ is running on its own.
One or more areas worth checking - see details below.
All mods running smoothly alongside Campaign++.
Game system replaced by another mod:
Open "Campaign++ Compatibility" in Mod Options to adjust settings or read area details.
{MODEL}: No other mod has replaced this system. Campaign++ features here are working normally.
{MODEL}: Load a campaign and the game will check this automatically.
{MODEL}: Another mod has replaced this system, but Campaign++ detected this and compensated automatically. No action needed.
{MODEL}: Another mod has modified this system (as an extension of the original). Campaign++ features here should still work, but check in-game if something feels off.
{MODEL}: Another mod has fully replaced this system. Some Campaign++ features that affect {MODEL_LOWER} may not be active. Check in-game if something feels off.
Worth a look - hover to read
Multiple mods alter the same gameplay calculation; final behavior may differ from either mod's standalone intent.
The other mod rewrites this calculation at a deep level. Worth checking in-game that both mods' effects feel right.
Both mods have logic that can change the outcome here. Everything likely works, but it's worth playing a few sessions to see if anything feels off.
Both mods add their own effect to the same daily calculation. The combined result is usually fine - you might just notice slightly stronger numbers than with either mod alone.
Both mods add their own contribution here independently. No conflict.
OLD - load a campaign to update

Shows whether this report reflects your current session. 'OLD' means you are viewing the report from before a campaign was loaded — mod list detection is complete but Core Game Systems have not been checked yet. Load any campaign and the report updates automatically.
Don't show this popup at startup
Turn this on if you don't want the compatibility report popping up every time you start a campaign. You can always read the report by clicking the button below.

This tells you at a glance whether Campaign++ is running smoothly alongside your other mods. "Runs fine" means both mods are active and their effects add up — no crashes, no missing features. "Check below" means one interaction is worth reviewing (hover those rows for plain-language details).

Some mods replace the game's internal calculation engines entirely. This checks whether the systems Campaign++ relies on are intact. "All normal" means everything is working. If an issue is found, hover the rows in the section below.
Load a campaign - full report pops-up

When you load a campaign, a compatibility popup appears automatically. It shows the mod list (already visible above) plus Core Game Systems — whether any other mod has replaced the internal calculation engines Campaign++ relies on. That Core Systems section only updates after a campaign is active, which is why it shows 'Start a campaign to check' until then. You don't need to come back here — the popup tells you everything on campaign load.

Reopens the same popup that appears automatically when you load a campaign. If 'Core game systems' above still reads 'Start a campaign to check', load a campaign first — the popup will appear on its own and the Core Systems section will fill in.
Refreshes when a campaign is started or loaded.
Battle casualties and siege aftermath drain settlement manpower pools. Raids deplete the bound settlement.
Kingdoms accumulate exhaustion from battles, raids, and sieges. High exhaustion slows regen and pushes the AI toward peace.
War exhaustion influences AI peace/war voting. Exhausted kingdoms become more willing to accept peace.
When exhaustion exceeds a critical threshold the kingdom is forced to make peace with one enemy.
Settlements suffer a regen penalty after raids, sieges, or conquest that decays over time.
Militia growth scales with the settlement's manpower ratio. Empty pools produce no militia.
Raid villages for slaves, sell them at town markets. Slaves boost town prosperity and construction.
Spend gold at friendly villages for hearth growth, notable relations, influence, and power.
Spend gold at friendly towns for prosperity growth, notable relations, influence, and power.
Non-bandit minor factions receive a daily stipend so they don't go bankrupt.
Garrison troops cost a reduced percentage of field wages (default 60%).
Castles generate elite troops from manpower and hold T4+ prisoners for conversion.
Removes the vanilla clan-tier bribe requirement for entering neutral castles.
Higher-tier troops are more likely to be wounded instead of killed.
Troop armor is factored into autoresolve battle outcomes.
Prevents clan annihilation when their kingdom is destroyed. Rescued clans seek mercenary service.
Owning many distant settlements reduces loyalty, security, and prosperity of far-flung holdings.
Raids cause persistent hearth, prosperity, security, and food penalties that decay slowly.
Castle regen above the local trickle is transferred from the nearest same-faction town, not created from nothing.
Spring/summer boost manpower regen, winter reduces it.
Kingdoms at peace get a manpower regen multiplier bonus.
Recruiting from matching-culture settlements costs less manpower.
Governors boost regen (Steward) and max pool size (Leadership).
In-game intelligence panel showing manpower, wars, factions, and more. Press M to toggle.
Yellow warning messages when a settlement's manpower drops below 25%.
[B1071] Internal ownership change at {SETTLEMENT} ({DETAIL}): {OLD_OWNER}->{NEW_OWNER} (kingdom: {OLD_KINGDOM}->{NEW_KINGDOM}). No conquest effects.
[B1071] Siege aftermath ({TYPE}) at {SETTLEMENT}: pool set to {APPLIED} ({RETAIN} retain, clamped from {NEWVAL}).
[B1071] Raid at {VILLAGE}: daily raid cap reached, no manpower drain.
[B1071] Raid not completed at {VILLAGE}: no manpower drain.
Manpower: cannot confirm — {POOL} needs {NEEDED} for {TROOP}, only {LEFT} left.
Manpower: Recruit All blocked — {POOL} needs {NEEDED} for {TROOP}, only {LEFT} left.
\nPrisoners T{NEXTTIER}+ (not enslaveable): {COUNT} → take to castle or ransom\n
⛓ {SETTLEMENT}: {COUNT} slave{PLURAL} in market. Daily: +{PROS} prosperity, +{CONS} construction.
({COUNT} T{TIER}+ kept — take to castle or ransom)

Converts all non-hero prisoners at Tier {MAXTIER} or below to Slave trade goods (1:1). Heroes and T{NEXTTIER}+ prisoners cannot be enslaved — take them to a castle for recruitment conversion or ransom at the tavern. Sell Slave goods via the town Trade screen. While Slave goods remain in the market, the town gains daily manpower, prosperity, and construction bonuses.
⛓ Enslaved {COUNT} T1–{MAXTIER} prisoner{PLURAL}.{KEPT} Slave goods in inventory: {INV}. Open the Trade screen to sell them to the market.
Only Tier 1–{MAXTIER} prisoners can be enslaved. Take T{NEXTTIER}+ to a castle for recruitment conversion or ransom at the tavern.
⚔ {SETTLEMENT}: {FREED} slave{PLURAL} manumitted (cap {CAP}). +{FREED} MP to town pool.
You already have an active investment in this town. You can invest again once the current patronage expires.
You need at least {COST} denars for a Modest investment.
You need {COST} denars for this investment.
You already have an active investment in this village. You can invest again once the current patronage expires.
You need at least {COST} denars for a Modest investment.
You need {COST} denars for this investment.
Pool multiplier at full security (100). E.g., 120 = safe settlement gets 120% bonus.
Do not force peace with an enemy currently besieging one of your owned towns/castles.
Legacy setting kept for save/config compatibility. Not used by current overlay UI.
Maximum elite troops regenerated per castle per day (at max prosperity). Actual amount scales linearly with prosperity. Default: 3.

Village raids accumulate persistent devastation (0-100) that decays slowly. Devastated villages suffer reduced hearth growth, and their bound town/castle suffers prosperity, food, and security penalties.
Adds compact instrumentation rows to the Current ledger tab for balancing/debugging.
Absolute minimum manpower regenerated per day (towns and fallback).
Enables the standalone Campaign++ overlay on campaign map.
Minimum in-game days between any two investments by the same AI lord (across all villages). Prevents carpet-bombing every village on a travel route. Set to 0 to disable. Default: 3.
When enabled, AI randomly picks from all affordable tiers instead of always choosing the highest. Default: true.

Maximum flat bonus manpower/day added when pool is at 0%. Scales linearly to 0 as pool approaches the depleted threshold. E.g., 2 = at 0% pool get +2/day bonus, at 7.5% get +1, at 15% get +0. Default: 2.
Gold cost to recruit a Tier 6+ prisoner. Default: 5000.
Minimum daily town regen as a percent of max pool (at 0 prosperity).
Logs structured WP1 telemetry events (regen snapshots, diplomacy rationale, truce/forced peace updates).
Gold cost for a Modest investment. Smallest tier with proportionally smaller bonuses. Default: 2000.
Influence gained from a Grand town investment (only if town is in your kingdom). Default: 50.
How strongly regen slows between soft-cap start and full pool. 0 disables slowdown effect.
Relation gained with each village notable from a Grand investment. Default: 10.

Tier-exponential scaling for troop wages. 0=Vanilla | 1=Light (T3+20%, T4+40%, T5+70%, T6+100%) | 2=Moderate (T3+50%, T4+100%, T5+160%, T6+250%) | 3=Severe (T3+70%, T4+150%, T5+250%, T6+400%). Heroes/companions and CaravanGuards are excluded. Applies to AI and player equally.
Minimum elite troops regenerated per castle per day (at zero prosperity). Default: 1.
Castle-specific regen floor. Represents peasant levies from bound villages and the castle's tiny civilian community. This trickle is organic (not drawn from towns). Default: 1.
Power added to each town notable from a Modest investment. Default: 5.

When a pool drops below a threshold, a bonus flat regen is added that scales inversely with fill ratio (emptier = faster recovery). Models limited Crown frontier investment — historically, devastated provinces took decades to recover.
Regen is multiplied by (1 - exhaustion/divisor). Higher = softer penalty. E.g., 200 means 100 exhaustion halves regen.
Average settlement manpower fill below which peace pressure kicks in. E.g., 40 = pressure starts when average pool is below 40%.
At or above this exhaustion score, forced peace checks become active.
Daily devastation decay while the village is in Normal state. Frozen during Looted/BeingRaided. Default: 0.5 (a single raid takes 50 days to fully heal).
Maximum random spread applied to daily regen output. E.g., 8 means ±8% around the computed regen value.
Relation gained with each town notable from a Modest investment. Default: 3.
Legacy reserved setting. Currently not used by active tooltip path.
Regen multiplier when security is 0. E.g., 80 = insecure settlement regens at 80%.
Per-point peace support bias when kingdom is in Rising pressure band.
Each village hearth × this = flat bonus to max pool. E.g., 0.10 means 1000 hearth village adds 100 max manpower.
Regen multiplier when well-fed. E.g., 100 = normal regen rate.
How many days the Generous investment hearth bonus lasts. Also serves as the cooldown. Default: 30.
Minimum pool size after tiny-pool scaling.
While under siege, regen is multiplied by this percent.
Shows slave economy and manpower messages in the game UI for player actions. Disable for normal play.
How long siege-related recovery penalties persist.
Security penalty per day applied to the bound town/castle at avg bound village devastation 100. Default: 1.5.
Base manpower pool for towns before economic scaling.
Manpower cost % when recruiting from matching culture. 75 = troops cost 75% of normal manpower (25% discount).
Master toggle. Enables castle prisoner auto-processing (T1-T3 enslaved to nearest town market, T4+ become recruitable after a waiting period) and the castle recruitment menu.
Regen multiplier when loyalty is 0. E.g., 50 = disloyal population halves regen.
[LEGACY — NOT USED] This setting is no longer active. Manpower cost is now flat per troop (BaseManpowerCostPerTroop). Kept for save compatibility.
Replaces hard exhaustion thresholds with graded Low/Rising/Crisis bands and hysteresis. Produces smoother diplomatic transitions.
Maximum total penalty applied to war declaration support in band mode. Replaces the hard -10000 override.
Depleted pools retain a higher percentage during conquest. Prevents ping-pong border castles from being permanently zeroed by repeated ownership changes.
At or above this exhaustion, AI kingdoms are prevented from starting new wars.
Adds small bounded randomness to volunteer production and post-shock recovery. Makes campaigns less predictable without wild swings.
How many days the Modest town investment lasts. Also serves as the cooldown. Default: 20.
Daily hearth growth bonus from a Generous investment. Default: 0.6.
When a kingdom's average settlement manpower falls below the threshold, AI clans gain extra support for peace outcomes (independent of war exhaustion).
How many days the Generous town investment lasts. Also serves as the cooldown. Default: 40.
Capturing an enemy noble (lord/lady) inflicts additional war exhaustion on the victim's kingdom.
Gold cost to recruit a Tier 4 prisoner. Follows the tier-exponential philosophy: elite troops are expensive to turn. Default: 1200.
Regen multiplier at full loyalty. E.g., 100 = loyal population regens at normal rate.
Near full manpower, regen progressively slows down instead of staying linear.
Master switch: logs ALL mod activity (manpower, slaves, diplomacy, patches) to the Bannerlord rgl_log file. Superset of every other debug toggle. Performance cost — disable for normal play.
Exhaustion gained by defending kingdom after siege aftermath.
Relation gained with each town notable from a Generous investment. Default: 6.
Max total manpower drained by completed village raids from the same pool in one day (% of max pool).
[LEGACY — NOT USED] Previously set how long the one-time construction bonus lasted after a slave sale. Superseded by the continuous market-based daily bonus in v3. Kept for save compatibility.
How long conquest-related recovery penalties persist.
Percentage chance that an AI lord will invest when entering an eligible village (after all other checks pass). Lower values reduce overall AI investment frequency. Default: 40.
Militia growth scales with manpower ratio. Depleted pools produce less militia.
How much exhaustion decays per day toward 0.
Retain % when pool is below the depleted threshold. Scales linearly between this and ConquestPoolRetainPercent based on fill ratio. Default: 85.
Additional support bias toward peace for each war above the pressure start count.
Relation gained with each village notable from a Modest investment. Default: 3.
At or above this exhaustion, AI support strongly favors peace outcomes.
Press the chosen key on campaign map to show/hide the overlay.
Pool fill ratio below which recovery penalty is halved. Default: 25.
Prosperity value at which pool reaches max scale. Typical towns: 2000-8000+, castles: 500-3500.
Prosperity penalty per day applied to the bound town/castle at avg bound village devastation 100. Default: 2.0.
Logs detailed reasons for forced-peace and war-gate decisions.

Master toggle. Enables the village investment (patronage) system — spend gold at villages for hearth growth, notable relations, influence, and notable power. AI lords also invest in their own villages. Default: true.
Maximum total bonus applied to peace decision support in band mode. Replaces the hard +200 override.

Higher-tier troops receive reduced simulated hit damage in autoresolve (T2=-6%, T3=-12%, T4=-18%, T5=-24%, T6=-30%). Lowers the probability of the fatal-hit gate firing per tick. Works together with tier survivability: fewer casualty checks AND better survival within each check.

Removes the clan-tier bribe (~800g) for entering castle lord's halls and allows entry to neutral castles even with slightly negative owner relations. Hostile castles and crime-based restrictions are unaffected. Needed for early-game prisoner deposit and recruitment. Default: true.
How long raid-related recovery penalties persist.
Governor Steward skill boosts regen; Leadership boosts max pool.
Base regen penalty applied after ownership change.
AI will not invest in villages with hearth at or above this value, focusing patronage on poorer villages that need it most. Set to 0 to disable (invest anywhere). Default: 600.

Maximum slaves a town can hold per point of prosperity. Excess slaves are manumitted (freed) daily and converted to manpower. E.g., at 0.02: a 3000-prosperity town can hold 60 slaves; 5000 prosperity = 100. Set to 0 to disable the cap. Default: 0.02.

Unified enslavement tier cap (player + AI parity). Prisoners at or below this tier can be enslaved: (1) player 'Enslave prisoners' at towns, (2) AI auto-enslave at towns, (3) castle auto-enslave to nearest market. Prisoners above this tier must be taken to castles for recruitment conversion or ransomed. Default: 3 (T1-T3 enslaved, T4+ recruitable).
Relation gained with the town owner's clan leader when investing in another clan's town. Default: 2.
Daily prosperity growth bonus from a Grand town investment. Default: 2.0.
Maximum peace-support bonus added when average manpower is at 0%. Scales linearly down to 0 as manpower approaches the threshold. Default: 100.
Devastation added to a village when its loot event completes. Two raids within the decay window stack. Default: 25.
How much governance strain decays per day toward 0. Lower = longer-lasting effects. Default: 0.3 (a +10 raid strain takes ~33 days to fully decay).
Pool multiplier when security is 0. E.g., 80 = insecure settlement has 80% of prosperity-scaled pool.
Maximum governance strain a settlement can accumulate. Default: 100.
Maximum daily town regen as a percent of max pool (at full prosperity).
Maximum exhaustion a kingdom can accumulate.

When enabled, AI lord parties visiting their own faction's castles recruit from both the elite pool and converted prisoners, paying gold per troop (same as player). No daily cap — lords fill to party limit. Default: true.
Power added to each village notable from a Modest investment. Higher power = higher-tier volunteers. Default: 5.

Percentage of the slave population lost per day (deaths, escapes, manumission). Without decay, slave populations grow forever. At 1%: 100 slaves lose 1/day. At 2%: 100 slaves lose 2/day. Creates equilibrium where inflow must match decay. Set to 0 to disable. Default: 1.0.
Regen multiplier at full security. E.g., 120 = safe settlement regens at 120%.
Maximum random spread applied to volunteer production probability. E.g., 10 means ±10% around the base value.
Food supply penalty per devastated village at devastation 100. Summed across all bound villages. E.g., 3 villages at dev 50 = 3 × 0.5 × 1.5 = 2.25 food/day lost. Default: 1.5.
Minimum number of days between automatic peaces for the same kingdom.
Daily hearth growth bonus from a Grand investment. Default: 1.0.
Steward skill is divided by this, then divided by 100 to get additional regen %. E.g., 100000 means 200 Steward adds +0.002 regen pct (0.2% of pool/day).
Daily hearth growth bonus from a Modest investment. Visible in village tooltip as 'Patronage'. Default: 0.3.
Militia growth multiplier when manpower is at 100%.
% of current pool retained when settlement changes hands (at full pool). Scales higher for depleted pools if dynamic conquest protection is enabled.
Regen multiplier when kingdom is at peace. E.g., 125 = 25% bonus.
Power added to each village notable from a Grand investment. Default: 20.

During a raid, 1 slave is added per this many village hearths, per loot event (same cadence as grain/clay appearing in the raid log). E.g., 300 = a 300-hearth village gives 1 slave per event; 600 hearths = 2. Villages below this threshold give 0 slaves per event. Default: 300 (historically balanced).
When enabled, recruiting a prisoner from a castle also drains the castle's manpower pool (same cost formula as normal recruitment). Creates a double gate: gold + manpower. Default: true.

Scales all daily town bonuses generated by slaves in the market (manpower, prosperity, construction). Higher = more effective slaves. Gold from selling slaves comes from the normal trade price in items.xml. Default: 1.5.
% of current pool drained when a bound village raid is fully completed.
Lowers forced-peace exhaustion threshold per extra major war.
Exhaustion gained by losing kingdom when a settlement changes hands.
Regen multiplier during winter. E.g., 75 = regen at 75% rate.
Forced peace only triggers when active wars exceed this number.
Base manpower pool for castles before economic scaling.
Relation gained with the village owner's clan leader when investing in another clan's village. Creates a diplomatic investment path. Default: 2.
How many days the Grand investment hearth bonus lasts. Also serves as the cooldown. Default: 45.
After any peace between two kingdoms, block re-declaration for this many days. Only active when truce enforcement is enabled.

Construction progress per slave per day (same scale as vanilla daily rate ~130-180). Visible as 'Slave Labor +XX' in the construction tooltip. At default effectiveness (1.5\u00d7): 0.5 \u00d7 1.5 = 0.75/slave. E.g., 100 slaves = 75 progress/day (capped). Default: 0.5.
Base regen penalty applied after siege aftermath.
Pool % at full prosperity. E.g., 100 = thriving settlement has 100% of base pool.
Power added to each town notable from a Generous investment. Default: 10.
Pool fill ratio where soft-cap slowdown starts. Example: 0.75 means slowdown begins at 75% full.
Influence gained from a Modest town investment (only if town is in your kingdom). Default: 10.
Base manpower cost for a tier-1 recruit.
Gold cost for a Modest town investment. Default: 5000.

When enabled, castle regen above the local trickle floor is transferred from the nearest same-faction town (not created from nothing). If the supply town is depleted, the castle starves. Historically, castle garrisons were rotated from towns by the strategos. Disabling reverts to legacy behavior where castles regen independently.
Higher values reduce the impact of village hearth sum on regen.
If enabled, player kingdom follows the same truce/no-war diplomacy gates as AI.
Maximum notable power allowed via investment. Investment power bonus is skipped if notable already at or above this level. Prevents absurdly high volunteer tiers. Default: 200.

Master toggle. Enables the town investment (civic patronage) system — spend gold at non-hostile towns for a daily prosperity bonus, notable relations, influence, and notable power. AI lords also invest in their own towns. Default: true.
Pool % when prosperity is 0. E.g., 30 = ruined settlement has 30% of base pool.
Prosperity penalty per day when strain is at the cap. Default: 1.0.

Maximum construction bonus per day from slave labour. Vanilla daily construction is ~130-180 for a typical town. Default: 150 (200 slaves at default settings max out the cap \u2014 roughly doubling build speed at peak).
Daily regen percent for non-town/non-castle settlements.
Gold cost for a Grand town investment. Highest tier with the strongest bonuses and longest duration. Default: 40000.
Shows a warning when your settlements' manpower drops below the threshold.
When enabled, AI randomly picks from all affordable tiers instead of always choosing the highest. Creates more natural variation — rich lords won't always pick Grand. Default: true.
Higher-tier troop casualties drain more manpower than low-tier ones (veterans are harder to replace). T1×1.0, T2×1.1, T3×1.25, T4×1.5, T5×1.75, T6×2.0.
Pools regen faster when your kingdom is at peace with all factions.
Base regen penalty applied after a completed raid.
Fallback pool size for settlements that are not town or castle.
Tracks which balance profile was last applied. Do not change manually — the mod migrates this automatically on update.
Influence gained from a Grand investment (only if village is in your kingdom). Default: 10.
Gold cost for a Generous investment. Middle tier with balanced bonuses. Default: 5000.
Percentage chance that an AI lord will invest when entering an eligible town. Default: 30.
How many days the Grand town investment lasts. Also serves as the cooldown. Default: 60.
Minimum in-game days between any two town investments by the same AI lord. Prevents carpet-bombing every town on a travel route. Set to 0 to disable. Default: 5.
Support penalty applied to declaring-war outcomes per exhaustion point.
% of max pool retained after choosing Show Mercy.
How many days the Modest investment hearth bonus lasts. Also serves as the cooldown — no re-investment until expired. Default: 20.
When enabled, AI lords invest in their own faction's towns when visiting them. Uses the same tier costs and provides the same bonuses. Default: true.
Gold cost for a Generous town investment. Default: 15000.
Influence gained from a Generous town investment (only if town is in your kingdom). Default: 25.
Buffer below band threshold before dropping back to lower band. Prevents rapid up/down oscillation.
When enabled, AI lords invest in their own faction's villages when visiting them (if they can afford it). Uses the same tier costs and provides the same bonuses. Default: true.

Food consumed per slave per day. Creates a natural economic cap on slave hoarding. At 0.05: 50 slaves = -2.5 food/day, 100 slaves = -5.0 food/day (roughly a village's output), 200 slaves = -10.0 food/day (severe). Historically, enslaved labourers received subsistence rations comparable to garrison troops (~0.04–0.06 food units). Set to 0 to disable. Default: 0.05.
Days a Tier 6+ prisoner must be held before becoming recruitable. Champion-tier troops resist the longest. Default: 21.
Shows a message when an AI lord invests in a village you own. Lets you see the patronage system working in your territory. Default: true.
Gold cost for a Grand investment. Highest tier with the strongest bonuses and longest duration. Default: 12000.
Spring/summer boosts regen, winter penalizes it.
Pool % below which a crisis alert is shown.
If tiny pools are enabled, poolSize = max(minTinyPool, poolSize / divisor).
Daily prosperity growth bonus from a Generous town investment. Default: 1.0.
Number of in-game days a rescued clan stays independent before seeking mercenary service. During this period they patrol near their home settlement. Default: 30.
Hard cap on daily regen as a percent of max pool. E.g., 2.0 means a 3000-pool town regens at most 60/day (~50 days to full).
Relation gained with each town notable from a Grand investment. Default: 10.
Maximum number of culture-based elite troops (T4-T6) a castle can hold at once. The pool regenerates daily from the castle's manpower. Default: 20.
Minimum daily castle regen as a percent of max pool (at 0 prosperity).
Pool fill ratio below which dynamic conquest protection kicks in. Default: 25.
Exhaustion gained by defending kingdom when a village is raided.
Maximum additional regen percent from hearth contribution.
Manpower drained from the castle's pool for each elite troop regenerated. Higher = slower elite regen in low-manpower regions. Default: 1.
Food stock value at which food modifier reaches maximum. Typical: 20-40.
Exhaustion level at which kingdom enters Crisis band (war declarations blocked, strong peace pressure). Default: 85.
% of max pool retained after choosing Pillage.
Garrison party total wage as a percentage of what the same troops would cost in a field party. 60 = garrison pays 60% (40% discount). 100 = no discount. Default: 60.
Scales down all pool sizes by the divisor below.

Master toggle. When a kingdom is destroyed, eligible clans are rescued instead of annihilated. They become independent factions during a grace period, then seek mercenary service with a culture-weighted kingdom. Default: true.
Each battle casualty drains this × 1 manpower from the party's home pool.
When pool is below the depleted threshold, recovery penalty effectiveness is halved. Prevents penalties from crushing already-ruined settlements that have nothing left to penalize.
AI will only invest when their gold exceeds the tier cost multiplied by this value. Higher for towns since costs are higher (e.g., 15 means a lord needs 600,000d for Grand). Default: 15.
Recruiting in matching-culture settlements costs less manpower.
Wars, raids, sieges, and conquests accumulate governance strain on settlements. High strain reduces loyalty, security, and prosperity — modelling the administrative cost of prolonged conflict.
Tracks per-kingdom exhaustion from battles, raids, and sieges. Reduces regen when high.
Pool fill ratio below which emergency regen activates. E.g., 15 = emergency regen when pool is below 15% full. Default: 15.
% of max pool retained after choosing Devastate.
Logs AI manpower consumption bands to the Bannerlord logs.
Vertical offset from the top of the screen for the overlay root panel. Increase this if game menus overlap the tab row.
AI will not invest in towns with prosperity at or above this value, focusing patronage on less prosperous towns. Set to 0 to disable. Default: 5000.
How many ledger rows are shown per page.
Regen multiplier during spring and summer campaign seasons.
Militia growth multiplier when manpower is at 0%.
Forced peace cannot end a war younger than this many days.
Maximum daily castle regen as a percent of max pool (at full prosperity).
Security penalty per day when strain is at the cap. Default: 2.0.
Exhaustion level at which kingdom enters Rising pressure band (elevated peace bias, softer war penalties).
Regen multiplier when starving (food stocks = 0). E.g., 50 = starving halves regen.
When active kingdom wars reach this count, extra peace pressure is applied.
Exhaustion added to a kingdom each time one of its nobles is captured in battle. Default: 3.
Minimum slave cap regardless of prosperity. Even a low-prosperity town can hold at least this many slaves before manumission kicks in. Default: 10.
After peace is made, block both kingdoms from declaring war again for the truce duration below. Disable if using AI Influence or another mod that controls its own war-declaration timing.
Leadership skill / this value multiplies max pool. E.g., 500 means 200 Leadership adds +40% to max.
AI will only invest when their gold exceeds the tier cost multiplied by this value. Higher = more conservative AI spending (e.g., 5 means a lord needs 60,000d to pick Grand). Default: 5.

Grants non-bandit minor factions a daily 'Frontier Revenue' stipend to prevent bankruptcy under tier-exponential wages. Mercenary clans receive less than unaligned factions (since mercs get contract pay).
If exhaustion is critically high, AI kingdoms will automatically end one war per cooldown period. Disable if using AI Influence or another mod that manages its own peace-forcing logic.
Per-point peace support bias when kingdom is in Low pressure band.

Commission the castle owner receives when another clan's prisoners are processed (enslaved or recruited). The depositing lord gets the remainder. Example: 30% fee on a 1,200g recruitment → owner gets 360g, depositor gets 840g. Default: 30.
Master toggle for raid/siege/battle/conquest manpower consequences.
Support bonus applied to make-peace outcomes per exhaustion point.
Daily prosperity growth bonus from a Modest town investment. Visible in the town tooltip as 'Civic Patronage'. Default: 0.5.

Controls how steeply slave prices decrease per unit of stock. Price factor = decayRate ^ stock, clamped at 0.1 (floor = 30d at base value 300). Lower values = steeper price drop. At 0.98 (default): stock 10 = ~245d, stock 30 = ~164d, stock 50 = ~109d, stock 80 = ~60d. At 0.96: stock 30 = ~88d, stock 50 = ~39d (floor). Default: 0.98.
Required for consistent player recruitment manpower consumption in current event flow. Keep enabled unless explicitly troubleshooting compatibility.
Horizontal offset from the left edge for the overlay root panel.

Higher-tier troops are more likely to survive as wounded rather than die in autoresolve. T1=+0%, T2=+5%, T3=+10%, T4=+15%, T5=+20%, T6=+25% flat bonus to survival chance on top of Medicine-based base rate.
Power added to each town notable from a Grand investment. Default: 20.
How strongly same-culture kingdoms are preferred when assigning mercenary service. Higher values make culture almost mandatory. At 2.0, same-culture kingdoms score 3× higher. Default: 2.0.
Influence gained from a Modest investment (only if village is in your kingdom). Default: 2.5.
Days a Tier 4 prisoner must be held before becoming recruitable. Represents the time needed to break resistance and negotiate loyalty. Default: 7.

Garrison parties pay a reduced wage relative to field troops. Historically accurate: garrison soldiers often received lower pay, supplemented by shelter and rations. Applies on top of vanilla perk and building discounts.
Relation gained with each village notable from a Generous investment. Default: 6.
Exhaustion gained by attacking kingdom after siege aftermath.
Scales how much depleted manpower amplifies exhaustion gains. At 1.0, a kingdom at 0% average manpower gets 2× exhaustion from each event; at 50% manpower it gets 1.5×. 0 = disabled.
Loyalty penalty per day when strain is at the cap. Scales linearly from 0 at strain 0. Default: 3.0.
Influence gained from a Generous investment (only if village is in your kingdom). Default: 5.
Power added to each village notable from a Generous investment. Default: 10.
War exhaustion directly affects AI kingdom war/peace decisions (DetermineSupport patches). Disable if using a dedicated AI diplomacy mod such as AI Influence to avoid double war-fatigue stacking.

Tier-exponential scaling for recruitment gold and upgrade gold. 0=Vanilla | 1=Light (+0/+15/+35/+65/+100/+150% T1-T6) | 2=Moderate (+10/+30/+75/+150/+250/+400%) | 3=Severe (+25/+75/+175/+350/+600/+1000%). Upgrade cost cascades automatically from hire cost (no double-stacking). Applies to AI and player equally. CaravanGuard/Mercenary/Gangster hire premiums are normalised to match regular soldiers at the same tier.
Hearth growth penalty per day applied directly to the village at devastation 100. Default: 2.0.
Daily income per clan tier for minor factions that are currently under mercenary contract. Lower than unaligned factions because they receive contract pay from their employer kingdom. Default: 250.

Master toggle. Enables: (1) village-raid slave acquisition, (2) prisoner enslavement at the town 'Enslave prisoners' menu, (3) daily town bonuses while Slave goods are present in the town's civilian market.
Days a Tier 5 prisoner must be held before becoming recruitable. Elite troops resist longer. Default: 14.
Maximum notable power allowed via town investment. Investment power bonus is skipped if notable already at or above this level. Default: 200.
Gold cost to recruit a Tier 5 prisoner. Default: 2500.
War exhaustion gains are amplified when a kingdom's average settlement manpower is depleted. Reflects the feedback loop: empty pools mean losses are felt more acutely.
Shows a message when an AI lord invests in a town you own. Default: true.
Floor for the combined daily prosperity penalty from Governance Strain + Devastated Hinterlands. Prevents runaway settlement death spirals when both systems stack. Default: -8.0.
Daily income per clan tier for minor factions NOT under any mercenary contract. Higher than mercenary stipend because they have no employer subsidising them. Default: 400.

Prosperity per slave per day (shown as 'Slave Labor' in Prosperity tooltip). At default effectiveness (1.5\u00d7): 0.0067 \u00d7 1.5 \u2248 0.01/slave. E.g., 100 slaves \u2192 ~1 prosperity/day. Default: 0.0067.
Applies temporary recovery penalties after raid/siege/conquest events, decaying over time.
Appends manpower info to settlement property tooltips in campaign UI.
Minimum daily regen percent of max pool after stacked stress penalties.
Construction bonus per slave per day
Max security penalty at full strain
Extra peace bias per major war
Notify when AI invests in your towns
Ignore if enemy besieges core fief
Min war days before forced peace
Max prosperity penalty at full strain
Notify when AI invests in your villages
Food consumption per slave per day
Max loyalty penalty at full strain
Campaign++ v{VERSION}: Balance settings updated to new defaults. Customize in MCM if desired. ({DETAILS})
After peace is made, block both kingdoms from declaring war again for the configured truce duration. Disable if using AI Influence or another mod that controls its own war-declaration timing.
Left-click a settlement to view its details.
Prisoners Ledger No imprisoned nobles found.
Both mods change how much food your towns have each day.
Both mods affect how fast villages grow or shrink.
Both mods affect town prosperity every day.
Both mods affect town security every day.
Both mods affect town loyalty every day.
Both mods affect how fast buildings are constructed.
Both mods affect whether you can enter a castle. Only one mod's rule applies at a time.
Both mods affect whether you can enter a lord's hall. Only one mod's rule applies at a time.
Both mods change how much you pay each troop per day.
Both mods change your total army upkeep bill.
Both mods change how much it costs to recruit troops.
Both mods influence when AI clans decide to go to war or sue for peace.
Both mods affect how often villages produce volunteers.
Both mods affect how many troops can be auto-recruited per day.
Both mods interact with how new recruits are processed.
Both mods affect how likely troops are to survive a battle.
Both mods affect how battle hits are calculated behind the scenes.
Both mods affect how much money clans earn each day.
